video_title: Inside Mankind & Dr. Reddy's: How "Generic" Built A Giant | WTF is Pharma?

Their products are household names, probably consumed by you at some time, and, of course, consumed by millions. So we decided to put a face to these stalwarts. [Meghana] So, how do you talk yourself into it everyday? I suppose, to prove it to my family, to my brother, that I'm worth something. So, that was only one, but... how can I make it to a level that I can start respecting myself? [Meghana] And I'm sure you've been through multiple phases of going through tough times. Erm, the biggest challenge I ever had was in our operations. This happened in the mid-- last decade, in the middle of the last decade, around. We had safety incidents where, you know, people died, and that really shook me up and really put me in a deep state of depression. So, that was a very difficult time for me. Mankind is led by Rajeev Juneja and his brother, with an annual turnover of about 40,000 crores. With numbers like these, many would coast, but not Rajeev or G.V. Their hunger keeps pushing the boundaries. Here's the thing nobody tells you. India's called the pharmacy of the world, but that's by volume. By revenue, we're barely a rounding error. We make the drugs, but someone else makes the money. So, who? And why? And more importantly, where does a 25-year-old find an opening in all of this? That's exactly what we asked. What is the business opportunity here? Can you guys think of anything else for the 25-year-old? There are 128 unicorns in India today, there is one in pharma drug innovation. [inaudible conversations] So, this is very casual. I was telling him earlier, Prasad, our audience is largely young people who want to start a business in your industry. So, we won't speak so much about your company, in particular. There's no controversy, there's no underlying, kind of, stuff. It's very much everything a 25-year-old needs to start a business in the pharma industry. So, think both of you are professors today, and you are teaching a class of young 25-year-olds. I am part of that class, so I'll keep asking you questions. Before we start, maybe you guys can tell me a bit about yourself. I know you guys already. You're very successful, popular people. But a little bit about your life and how you got to be where you are. Would you like to go first? [Rajeev] I can go first. I don't have any issues. My name is Rajeev Juneja, and I have one wife. [laughter] And I have five kids, three daughters, two sons. Daughters means, I mean, they are daughter-in-laws, and one granddaughter. And I'm a college dropout because circumstances were like this. And number one, I was not interested in studies. So that's why I got an excuse that there's a compulsion in the family, and the money is not there. So, I used to go to my brother's medical store. There, I learned how to run a pharmacy. Like people go to Grandma's place in summer vacation, I used to go to that medical store for two months. So, eight, nine months, I worked over there. That was from tenth class to PUC Part-1. Then, one day, I said, "I want to be a medical rep." I became a medical rep in my brother's company. Worked ten years over there. And there, I did a lot of things. How market really works, how sales reps, how companies, how doctors operate, how this market operates. I never knew that. And then I became a medical rep in a local kind of a company, it was. Local means, in the sense, a black-and-white company. Not even colours were there in boxes and visual aids or literatures. So, when you work in that kind of a company, if you will not become a brand yourself, company will not run, where failure rate is 80%, 90%. Every time just... You become so thick-skinned, at the same time, sensitive. It was somewhat of an oxymoron. Same time you are stubborn, or thick-skinned, at the same time, you are very, very sensitive. Government doesn't like it because it's a pharmaceutical product. -It has serious side effects. -Correct. And you can't promote it to people who are gullible. Muscle loss, problems in the bones, other-- So many issues are there. People are not basically looking for those side effects. I'll just tell you why I find this difficult to find an opportunity for a 25-year-old. -This is a deep science business. -Right. You have to have technical skills, all of that. Right. Marketing is important, but the product is much more important. -Right. -And you need to have the right products and the marketing overlay-- But the 25-year-old is not making the product. He's buying it from you. He's building a brand on top of it. You know, if there was any arbitrage in that, I would have done it myself. There's many cases where there are arbitrages and people do it. He will not be doing it. See, I mean, this product, please understand that this product has to be prescribed only. Even if a patient is buying it, right? He has to get the prescription of a doctor. So, I'll tell you an arbitrage which came in the US. They have something called compounding doctors, compounding pharmacies. They actually buy the GLP powder from China and they mix it into an injectable, load it, and ship it to customers. All for products which are under-patent, but are under-supplied. So, both Lilly and Novo had supply issues initially as the demand rose much higher than what they projected. Right. And these guys made billions of dollars. -Yeah? -Young guys. All they had to do was contract manufacturing of some product, buy the ingredients, putting it together. As a pharmaceutical company, we don't want to do it because we are scared of them suing us. These guys made huge money. I know a guy who sold more than a million dollars' worth of product at 80-90% gross margin. -But you can't do that in India. -No, you can't do it in India. There's a rule in the US that if there is a shortage, the pharmacist can compound it. Another question. If you guys are operating at 20-25% EBITDA, which is quite high, why doesn't the-- like, you were talking about my chain, why doesn't the guy putting in the money to research take over this profit centre himself? So, in many countries, they do. So, I'll tell you the yin and yang, in what context I told you that. The innovator is making-- is spending billions of dollars, developing a product, launching it globally, -and making a lot of money. -Right. -Through a monopoly rights. -Right. Patent rights give him monopoly rights for extended periods of time. Usually, it's less than ten years. After accounting for all the time it takes to develop, seven years, eight years. And the profits are massive. They're selling at huge prices. And that is the innovator's business. So, he keeps on innovating and then doing this. As a generic company, our approach is to get the patent life as short as possible. So, we'll find a loophole to get in early, challenge the patents and say they're not valid patents. So, we fight that. So, we try to shorten their useful life of their monopoly thing, they fight to extend it by suing us. There's a constant legal battle. This is the yin and yang. The people doing the generic cannot sustain without an innovator there, because if there's no innovator, there's no generic. And if we don't bring their patents down, they'll have monopolies forever and they won't innovate fast enough. -Right. -So, in that sense, the generic industry and the innovator industry are the yin and yang of innovation. Why do Indian pharmacies, pharmaceutical companies, spend only 3-5% of budget on R&D but the Western ones spend 20? I think the nature of R&D we do is different. We do process development. We develop processes for products which are known and that doesn't require a huge amount of money. But some of us do research, we do spend. Our numbers are double digit in R&D. The bigger companies are spending. Sun spends, Zydus spends. All of these are double digit, more or less. The Indian companies alone don't have to do it because if they're focused only on India, they're doing branded generics business. For our India business, we only develop products at a much lower cost, 2-3%, 5%. If you're basically spending money on R&D, you're supposed to have a global presence. Then only it will make some kind of a sense. Only from India? Not possible. Is that the plan in the future? Like, as a brand, selling globally? See, all of us, we want to increase our margins. When you're sitting on the generic side, the innovation side looks so sexy. The margins are so good, the monopoly rights. Swimming in money. A company like Pfizer is, what, 60-70 billion dollars in revenue. Merck is even bigger. That's bigger than the Indian industry. It is bigger than the industry. So, if you want to go to that scale, you have to be an innovator. But innovation and generics, they are not a continuum. People like to say, "We're moving up the value chain. We're going to innovation." It's a very different business. Yeah, but just look at the industry. Just let me complete this train of thought, then you can come in. Generics is something that you know, you develop processes, you file, you get-- It's all very predictable. Discovery research and finding a novel therapeutic is a long, arduous process. Very high failure. So you're moving in from a business which has a large number of small, tiny bets to a business which has a very small number of very large bets. -Right. -The risk profile is very different. Right. So, to move there from here is a big challenge. And that's the challenge, that's something that we're all facing. It requires much bigger capital. And we can't leave this business because this is our bread and butter. And we need to keep funding that and then also create a new business. It's very demanding in terms of science and investment. This might be a stupid question. But when, say, whoever created GLP-1 created it, if you got the product, you can't reverse engineer it? -That's what we do. That's what we did. -Right. That's why we launched. That's the generic. That takes a long time? No, it takes two, three years, we can do it. GLP-1 was a little more complicated product, so maybe four years. Otherwise, I mean, the day it got, I mean, off-patented... -We had 30 people. -...the next... -...after one week, it was launched. -[Prasad] Yeah. When you say off-patent, does it mean they disclose what the formulation is? No, no, no. We don't need that. Patents have all the details. Patent expiry date in India was '26. So, in the patent only, the formulation will be there? Yeah. But we don't even need that. We can reverse-engineer from the product itself. In this case, the Chinese are much better at biological processes. And the GLP-1 they make, the semaglutide they make, is from a biologic root. So they're able to scale it much, much better than Indians. Indian companies are largely in synthesis, which is an inefficient way of making semaglutide. What else? Meanwhile, can you guys think of anything else for the 25-year-old? There are 128 unicorns in India today. There is one in pharma drug innovation, which is called Molbio. -Molbio? -Yeah. -Huh? -[man] Testing kit. -Testing kit. -The diagnostic company. That's not even... It's not in the same-- Yeah, but it is innovative, what they did. The diagnostics they developed were much better and easier to-- tailored for the Indian market. I mean, if you just look at this, you're talking about the R&D side in India. Why companies are spending less, because we are now coming to the cusp of that place where companies have become a bit serious. Why so? Because slowly and gradually, what basically is happening, government-side, regulatory is becoming a bit stringent. [Nikhil] Right. More regulatory will become stringent. So, naturally, one day would come when every company, factory, would be manufacturing a quality product. Right. Then, the kind of advantage a branded company has, like I said, top 50 companies have more than 75% market share, will lose that gleam, right? Because why some patient goes to a branded company, a known company? Because there is a trust that if I buy, I'm sure this is the right kind of a product. On the other side, because we keep hearing a lot of cases are there. I mean, that problem happened, this problem happened. And, sometimes, the problem is not because of the drug which is there in the syrup or tablet. It is in the excipient which was used. Some kind of a reaction happened, some problem came. So that kind of an expertise are not there with those factories. Right. So that fear is now there, at least if you ask me, in our head, that maybe what would happen after 5-10 years' time. [Nikhil] Yeah. So why not now invest and come to some kind of a level where we can rely on our own R&D? So this is a big-- See, a company like us, we spend 2.75% on R&D. And what we've decided now, every year, we're supposed to increase it by 0.5%. -Right. -And make it to highest level possible. Then, how smartly you use it, what kind of a talent you bring-- I mean, if you bring a right talent, they bring more talent. What is the CDMO industry? Is there an opportunity there? Yeah, CDMO is doing well. The multiples are like hospital multiples now. Right, what does it do? They do-- They work for large innovative companies to either do FTE-based research, full-time equivalence-based research, developing molecules, discovering libraries of compounds, and all that. Medicinal chemistry or even biologics. That is one part. That is the CD part. Manufacturing is actually producing the product. Since these companies have very good margins, and, for them, security of supply is much more important than cost at that point. If you have a strong manufacturing infrastructure, quality systems, they will outsource the manufacturing to India. On top of this, the uncertainty with China, not just the tariff, but also there is-- what was the act called? Uh, the BIOSECURE Act. For their country's supply chain security, they passed this BIOSECURE Act which encouraged companies to move out of China. So, there is a whole thing called China Plus One strategy. That's an opportunity for Indian companies. All the CDMOs are growing very well. Your own city has a nice one called Anthem Biotech. It's again a startup. -And these are small companies. -Anthem is big now. -But someone small can start-- -They were small once. They started with some small amount of capital, rented premise. Today, they're-- I don't know-- The market cap is what? Anthem? Maybe 20,000 crores, something like that. So, this is a good success. -But someone small can try it. -They can try. These guys were scientists who were working in Kiran's labs. They came out and started somewhere and built a sizeable business. Not 25 years old. [all laughing] You need to have some kind of a scientific experience. You can start when you're 25. You can work your way through for the next ten years. Anything we missed, guys? Like, can you think of anything? -Covered a lot. -Yeah, most of my research is done. But if you were a college teacher and you were teaching a class, and you covered all that we covered today, what do you think we missed? I'm going to skip this. It's sugar, no? No, it's protein, creatine... -No sugar? -...and protein powder. I'm diabetic, so I won't try. Just ask Pragati, no? -Sam? -[Sam] Yeah? Just ask Pragati if there's sugar in this. But there's protein powder with creatine and these nuts are really cool because-- -Eat a bit. -[Prasad] Pecan nuts? -It would be a healthy one. -They're called butter pecan nuts. Okay, I'll try. And the base is tofu. Okay. Yeah, so, what did we miss is the question, right? In every part of our business, there are startups. There have been startups. Like? Somebody who's starting at the age of 25, startup, and some kind of a belief system, in the next five years' time, we'll make a mark. These are time-taking things. A lot of patience. And once you basically talk about R&D, you talk about patience. The first thing is that. Mankind is a good example of success. Yeah. They took the traditional business model and disrupted it. They were seen as a disrupter back then. We were always telling our sales reps, "See how much the Mankind guy is doing. How come we are not growing at that pace?" They said, "What they do, we can't do." I said, "Why not?" "Because they're this, they're that. Our compensation systems are different." All kinds of excuses. But you came and turned it around. So there is something there to disrupt in all of our businesses. Is there a patient feedback company somebody can start? Say, for example, I'm taking your Manforce whatever product, or your GLP-1. What if somebody comes in and builds a company where there's an application where the patient every day reports back on video to an AI how he's feeling and I can sell that data back to you? [exhales deeply] Never heard about this. Worth thinking. But why a patient will give a feedback? My question is that. What is the attraction to him? What will you pay to him? Maybe like, I don't know, 50 rupees a day. So, question is that. I mean, will it be very expensive or not? And how will you monetise? The point is, I mean, somebody is taking a medicine, the effect comes out a few days, it takes time. [Nikhil] Fair. I would say there's still opportunity here. I think we haven't figured out, because we have our lens of large company, large size revenue streams. You talked a lot about AI everywhere. I'll tell you, honestly, one thing which always haunts me-- -Let me finish the AI part? -[Rajeev] You go ahead. So, the AI part is a big deal for us. And we are not equipped to do it. Whether it's in our sales force management, targeting doctors, all of that can be driven much more efficiently through agents and things like that. Our processes, R&D processes can improve. Now we are able to do in silico experiments, in silico organoids. These are all areas where there can be services to pharma, which we don't have the capability to do. And we can't attract the talent. A smart AI guy doesn't want to work for a, you know, pharmaceutical company. So you would be willing to outsource that? It's actually more than outsource. It's really a core process for us if we can do that. My idea is very simple. I mean, approximately 1.5-2% of medicines get expired. Where do these expired medicines go? In some place to be properly burned... -Destroyed, yeah. -...destroyed. Right? And this is totally unorganised. And there lies a scope. -Yeah? -Yeah. So you'll pay money for somebody to take it from you and destroy it? Making sure that it does not come back in the market. That's a big problem, right? Counterfeits coming back. Yeah. See, example, every company basically has a pledge to take the expiry back and give the money back to the consumer. But what? I take the expiry. I burn it. I give to some agency. It does not burn. It sells in the market. It again comes back. Can some company give this kind of a guarantee properly? I mean, using technology, video, AI, whatever, that I am burning it. I give you guarantee. I give you a certificate. All these things are there. Because, apart from this, government agencies are involved. Because when you burn some chemical, you pollute the area. So, in a very remote place, special red zones, you can open that. I tell you honestly, this is a good one. I mean, see, people always look at the glamorous side. Let's do this. Let's do what is going on. Why don't you look at some basic, unglamorous thing... which is basically the need of the hour? I mean, sometimes you find that unglamorous things are more profitable. [Nikhil] Yeah. Everyone runs towards glamour. I mean, looking at your idea about expiry medicines, in the US, I saw an NGO which does this. The shelf life is less than some months. They take your medicines free of cost, and distribute to patients who need them. And it's not a business, but it's philanthropic. If we do it here, it will be sold again. We need to have controls. -But, in the US, this is-- -What controls? I'm giving it to an NGO, NGO is selling back, getting the money, and then using the money somewhere else. Yeah, but I'm just saying this happened in the US. I'm telling you, the idea is not bad. The idea is very good. I mean, six months before a product gets expired, it goes out of circulation. What to do? And again, I mean, for example, let me talk about the expiry product. Very basic. Basic. Just a common sense. At a 100 milligram, when it reaches to 95, it is expired. Right? What if a poor patient takes 95 plus something? A bit more? Efficacy is 95? [Rajeev] Who has no access to medicine. It's a very wild thing I'm saying. You know, this can go sideways if you do that. -I'm not saying-- -Can a product become poison? -Yes, absolutely. -It can? See, a milligram can cure you. That means a milligram can also hurt you. My question is-- -Safety is important. -You can tell me. If, for example, shelf life of a drug is two years. Right? Example, if I take something after two years and two months. I wouldn't do that. I'll tell you why. I'll give you my logic. The shelf life was arrived at with a rigorous statistical analysis of all the data that the drug is good only for this time. You don't want to violate that. And then, the whole thesis of how you do business goes off. Six months before that? Expiry? That's what I'm saying. When there's short dating, people are donating the medicines. -That is... -Somebody can guarantee that? You can't test the drug to see if it's okay? Like a two-and-a-half year old batch-- See, the government also needs some rules to enforce, right? They have given you two years' lifetime based on the data you submitted. You can always extend it if you produce more data and then say that, "You please extend it." And then, you can do it. But without doing that, you can't do anything. I think that's not the right thing to do. [chuckles softly] I talk from a business point-of-view, you talk from a science point-of-view. Not science. This is not science. This is rules. But it's nice. You both have a good contradiction. I talk from the idiot point-of-view. [all laughing] Anything else that you can think of for somebody young to start, or think about? You see, I tell you honestly, when people start a business, the problem is what? They copy a set formula. That this is the way these companies have become successful. And that is totally wrong. Never ever follow a set formula. Example, I'll give you. Just leave it here, it's fine. I'll leave it there. I'll keep it here. The set formula was launch all-India. We said no. Only western UP. Proof of concept in western UP. Then expand slowly and gradually. The idea of following somebody's set formula is always wrong. I say, launch in a city. Very less risk. -That's a good lesson. -Yeah? Very good. See, first of all, you become a lion-- Be a lion in your own territory first. That's a good strategy, actually. We believe always focused approach, 100%. Find a mini market, succeed, and then plan. Always. And go in a market where nobody's going. Because there's always some place which can be a competition-free zone. There must be something somewhere. I mean, just look at this e-commerce. When e-commerce came... So, first, it was Amazon and Flipkart. Everybody came, flopped, closed down. Then somebody said, "No, no. I'm only focusing, narrowing down myself to cosmetics or clothing." -Right? -Yeah. Or something else. Then somebody said, "No, all this is useless. Quick commerce." Did they follow? They took the inspiration, but created a different path. So, any time, I mean, looking-- What is visible should not be seen, should not be followed ever. That we always follow. You know, if I had a bunch of genius AI guys, I would create a lab in my office for them and say, "This is the company. This is my cost structure. These are my cycle times. These are my problems." It's not a pharma business, but it's an AI business. If you had to ask your kids to join your business or to build something similar, would you? I would ask them to learn the business. And the business is of a much larger size. Then they will be able to run until they're in the business for a decade. And I don't think they have that kind of patience. One of my sons is in diagnostic business. The second has been put on the R&D side. I know my priorities. If I say that next focus area of ours would be R&D, so I'm putting my bets over there. Then diagnostics, because going forward, I mean, this is going to play a big role. -Diagnostics? Yeah? -Home diagnostics. I mean, we sell Prega News. It's what? It's a kit just to check your pregnancy. -How much we sell? -[Nikhil] Yeah? 300 crore rupees' worth. I think the game changer will be when you can take blood on your own. [Rajeev] Yeah. -Somebody was trying that. -You can still take a drop. People do it for measuring their blood sugar. Microfluidics are coming in, which can give a lot more data with just that one mark. Like that Theranos. Theranos was a fraud, but that's the idea. -[Nikhil] But the principle. -Yeah. -That's possible? -It's possible. Like, in one drop of blood, I can find out my liver enzyme, my cholesterol, all of that? There are people trying to do cancer with that one drop. Yeah. Some screening. I think that's it. We recorded for four and a half hours, which is a long time. [all laughing] I'm not sure you got something your audience would be interested in. No, no, it's interesting. See, the thing is, it's not even that, "Here is the option. You should go do it." It's also figuring out what can't be done. Yeah, yeah. It's useful. So if you don't think of it as a fast-built business, this is a great business to me. But you should have a decade to build it. Yeah. A good example is Anthem. 32,000 crores. The guy was an employee of... Any last words? You guys want to say something to the audience? [Rajeev] There's nothing shortcut. I mean, you have to be very, very patient if you want to come in this line. And the best way to start your business is first to take some experience in some area where you want to basically focus later on. It's always good. I mean, without risking your money... -Right. -...you're getting experience. Fine. I'd say that... [clears throat] The pharmaceutical business is a great business for India. If you take out the IT sector, this is a business which takes products to all over the world. It's sold as branded products in many parts of the world. And I think... huge impact. So I'm very proud of what we have done, and I think there's more that we can do. Pharmacy of the world doesn't happen very fast. We're called-- Of course, it's, albeit, generic pharmacy of the world. But, you know, it's a good foundation to build on. I think the next frontier is finding new drugs. I feel, I mean, next ten years will be very interesting. Extremely interesting for Indian pharma industry. Because once you know that-- If you basically are cognisant to the future challenges, you take proactive actions. Super. Thank you so much for doing this. -I hope you didn't get too bored. -[chuckles] No, no. Thank you very much. -Thank you. -Thanks, all. -Thank you very much. -You got us in the middle of May. This was a different kind of a dinner podcast. [Nikhil] Yeah. It's very, very college-like. -Yeah. Chit-chat and talk. When we were researching you, we learnt that you like taking photographs of birds. -Yes. -We would have never expected it. What is the story? From my childhood, I was always interested in nature. -Hmm. -It's not photography is my interest, nature is my interest. -Hmm. -So... One of my friends who lives down the road was a businessman and a very good ornithologist. So, he introduced me to birds and that's how I started birding. Then it became a passion. I published some books and I also go on trips for birding. What's your idea of a fun weekend? Saturday, Sunday, what would you do? I don't if it's about fun, but if I had an ideal weekend, -I would go to the farm or something. -Hmm. When the weather is not like this, but it's nice. And I'll shoot some pictures, I'll visit my parents-- my mother, my father has passed away-- my mother, spend some time with her, and then, maybe in the evening, meet some friends. That's very interesting. -And for you? -For me? -The idea of fun? -Fun weekend. Friends, drinking. -Mm-hm? In Delhi? -Any place. [laughter]

transcript_segments.text: [narrator] India makes medicines that heal billions of people across the world.
Most of them have no idea who's behind it.
After much demand, we brought together two of India's pharma giants,
Dr Reddy's and Mankind, to help you, the young entrepreneurs of this country,
grasp the industry's nuances.
Their products are household names, probably consumed by you at some time,
and, of course, consumed by millions.
So we decided to put a face to these stalwarts.
[Meghana] So, how do you talk yourself into it everyday?
I suppose, to prove it to my family, to my brother, that I'm worth something.
So, that was only one, but...
how can I make it to a level that I can start respecting myself?
[Meghana] And I'm sure you've been through multiple phases
of going through tough times.
Erm, the biggest challenge I ever had was in our operations.
This happened in the mid-- last decade, in the middle of the last decade, around.
We had safety incidents where, you know, people died, and that really shook me up
and really put me in a deep state of depression.
So, that was a very difficult time for me.
[narrator] Reddy Pharmaceuticals is headed by G. V. Prasad
and brings in roughly three billion in revenue.
Mankind is led by Rajeev Juneja and his brother,
with an annual turnover of about 40,000 crores.
With numbers like these, many would coast, but not Rajeev or G.V.
Their hunger keeps pushing the boundaries.
Here's the thing nobody tells you.
India's called the pharmacy of the world, but that's by volume.
By revenue, we're barely a rounding error.
We make the drugs, but someone else makes the money.
So, who? And why?
And more importantly, where does a 25-year-old
find an opening in all of this?
That's exactly what we asked.
What is the business opportunity here?
Can you guys think of anything else for the 25-year-old?
There are 128 unicorns in India today, there is one in pharma drug innovation.
[inaudible conversations]
So, this is very casual.
I was telling him earlier, Prasad, our audience is largely young people
who want to start a business in your industry.
So, we won't speak so much about your company, in particular.
There's no controversy, there's no underlying, kind of, stuff.
It's very much everything a 25-year-old needs
to start a business in the pharma industry.
So, think both of you are professors today,
and you are teaching a class of young 25-year-olds.
I am part of that class, so I'll keep asking you questions.
Before we start, maybe you guys can tell me a bit about yourself.
I know you guys already. You're very successful, popular people.

Would you like to go first?
[Rajeev] I can go first. I don't have any issues.

[laughter]

Daughters means, I mean, they are daughter-in-laws,

I'm... I'm new in Delhi.
2004-2005, we came to Delhi.

And number one, I was not interested in studies.
So that's why I got an excuse that there's a compulsion in the family,
and the money is not there.

I used to go to that medical store for two months.

I became a medical rep in my brother's company.

-It's been that long? -Yeah, 30 years.
It's been a while.
One second. Don't hold him by the chain.
Don't... Leave him open.

is because of whatever, I'll say, destiny dictates.

what you hate becomes a big lesson for you.

buying goods from wholesale market...
at the age when my friends were enjoying.

How market really works, how sales reps, how companies,
how doctors operate, how this market operates.

if you will not become a brand yourself,
company will not run,
where failure rate is 80%, 90%.

Same time you are stubborn, or thick-skinned,
at the same time, you are very, very sensitive.

these two enable that...
